name: Cova d'en Domingo
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint in Génova. Follow the path towards Coll des Pastors. At the pass, take the right path for about 77 m until it widens at a cistern, then take the first right path (careful — vegetation increasingly hides it).
description: A natural cave in the Coma Fosca ravine historically modified for human use as a shelter. It consists of a single chamber with a notably flat floor, decorated with stalactites, flowstones, and a beautiful group of columns reminiscent of the Coves Petites de Capdepera. The cave functions as a sinkhole during heavy rains, and is considered a Natural Site of Interest.
full_description:
The cave in question is located inside the Sa Coma Fosca torrent, to the left of the path. Before reaching it, if approaching from the Coll des Pastor, we will leave to our right the avenc of the same name, which is protected with a metal mesh to prevent hunting dogs from falling into it.

The path that runs through the interior of Sa Coma Fosca toward the Barranco des Sec has been abandoned by the landowners along with other paths on the same estate, in order to prevent the constant passage of cyclists through certain parts of the property. Each passing day it becomes harder to travel along it as the vegetation progressively covers it, and if it were not for the force of the water — since the path runs along the torrent — it would no longer exist.

Finding the cave is not difficult at all since it is right next to the path and the entrance dimensions of 1.70 x 1 m make it impossible to miss. The cave's orientation is 315° NW. As soon as we approach the entrance, we immediately notice that this natural cave has been adapted over time by humans, as the entrance has been modified to allow it to be closed with a door that no longer exists.

What stands out is the flat floor of much of the cave's single chamber, since most caves in the area begin descending immediately. Logically, the flatness of the floor is partly due to human hands, as they presumably used it as a shelter during work periods in the area — evidence of this being the ceramic remains found inside the cave.

I could not verify whether columns once existed in the center of the chamber that were torn out to leave more free space, although at the back of the chamber a fairly thick column lies on the ground. As I was saying, the cave consists of a beautiful chamber with the occasional compartment. Except for the left side of the cave which is not concretioned, the rest of the cave is: starting with a stalactite next to the right side of the cave which is possibly a decapitated column, a flowstone covers all the rest of the cave except for the previously mentioned part. A group of beautiful columns at the back of the chamber stands out, which at some point reminded me of the Coves Petites of Capdepera.

At the back and to the right of the chamber, one must descend a little more than two meters using only hands to reach a place that seems to function as the cave's sinkhole, where a pile of sediments dragged there by the force of water can be found. It should be remembered that the path by which one accesses the cave runs through the interior of the Sa Coma Fosca torrent, so it is not at all unusual for flash floods to occur that seep into the cave's interior. In fact, my first visit to the cave took place after heavy rains and everything seemed to indicate that a large quantity of water had entered.

Some activity is detected in certain parts of the cave, though not in the interesting group of columns that decorates it.

As for the cave's fauna, I could only observe inside it a single specimen of bat in the highest part of the ceiling, but in several places one could see efflorescences of droppings from these animals.

There are many caves and pits in the area, so we only have to choose one to complete the day if the visit to Cova d'en Domingo turns out to be too short.

Cova d'en Domingo is considered a Natural Site of Interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.
